edibility: Sulfur Tuft is inedible and toxic, containing toxic steroids fusciculol E and F. The extremely bitter taste serves as a natural deterrent. While fatalities are rare, poisoning can cause severe symptoms including gastrointestinal distress, temporary paralysis, and distorted vision.
taste: Intensely bitter and acrid. Even small amounts cause strong bitter sensations; the mushroom's main natural warning is its extreme bitterness which should deter consumption.
search: Sulfur Tuft grows in dense clusters on hardwood stumps and decaying wood, appearing year-round in temperate regions. Identification features include greenish-yellow gills, bright yellow cap margin, and the clustered growth pattern. Look-alikes include Honey Fungus (Armillaria mellea) which is edible when cooked, and Galerina marginata which is also toxic; distinguished by taste testing and gill color.

toxicity.symptoms: Gastrointestinal distress including nausea, vomiting, explosive diarrhea, abdominal pain, excess protein in urine (proteinuria); severe cases may include temporary paralysis, impaired vision, and collapse.
toxicity.treatmentWindow: Up to 10 hours from consumption; early supportive care and decontamination (activated charcoal) within first few hours if ingestion is recent
toxicity.antidote: No specific antidote; treatment is supportive care including fluid replacement for dehydration from gastrointestinal symptoms, antiemetics for vomiting, and monitoring for complications. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
